author: @Imasurvivor2
text: I don’t get it. What’s the goal of this device?

replies.author: @AdamBechtol
replies.text:
I had no clue either, reading some comments maybe the wind out the bottom is supposed to accumulate from a bunch of leaves and drive a turbine. 
But more likely he was just interacting with a poster to encourage their ideas. That's my guess anyway.

author: @ZsOtherBrother
text:
Simple, beautiful, modular... genius!!
When I saw this my first thought was a modular wall of these.
A honeycomb design would be nice, with the combined air flow from the modules' outlets at the top running through "pipes" along the edges of the modules below them. Then again, diamond shaped modules should make air flow much less turbulent then hexagons, (straighter lines).
To make the "pipes", the edges of each module could be shaped as half a pipe (lengthwise) so when they are "clicked" together they'd form complete pipes. 
The top and sides would need to be completed into pipes as well, which can also be done with modular pieces.

author: @AB-C1

Wonder if a wind vein of some kind added would allow it to face the wind constantly one way. Then perhaps it could be modified to direct the wind from there into it to increase efficiency? 
Cheers from London 👍

author: @davidkennedy6641
text: I think these devices are rife with energy loss problems, there is plenty of turbulence created in changing the direction of the air flow and a load of friction sending the air down pipes.  Also, more turbulence at any point where down pipes join.  This is a matter is simplicity imposes less loss, hencevtha traditional turbines where energy is virtually directly generated at it's source.  On the other hand, a wind tree would look good so what the heck, go ahead and make one.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am afraid that's my thought a wind tree would look awesome! lol

replies.author: @elizabethmckinney7182
replies.text: I think that's the only point. Aeromine and the Darwin wind capture device, they aren't about efficiency. They're about aesthetics, because nobody wants standard turbines mid city

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: There is a british wind turbine very similar to the Darwin wind turbine that outputs a lot of energy, but it has a VAWT in the middle of it. I don't recall its name now, but it's output was huge. It seemed to be efficient but it was also very big so I don't know. Still trying to capture wind from all directions at the same time without interference (VAWTs interfere with themselves for example) and being able to use many of them at the same time may beat other solutions even if the efficiency is lower.

author: @zfolwick
text: this hardly requires a 3d printer.  This basically is just a box with a hinge at the top, a hole through 2 opposing faces and the bottom, and said hinge closing a flap.  Make the box slanted and the hinged door closes tightly.  With a crosscut sled this could be mass-produced out of PVC, veneer wood, or hobby wood rather easily (if a bit tedious).

@ThinkingandTinkering I think the 3d printer is a great plan for making prototypes!  I just don't have one.  They are superior than a woodworkers shop, because everything in a woodworkers shop is out to take a finger off 😀

I really appreciate the reply.  I've been loving your builds and have been tinkering with heating my workshop with planter pots from your videos.

author: @michaelwarbon
text: Difference in heat and density causes wind so why not do the same.  Run a pipe through the ground and have it exit in a chamber where it is painted black.  The heated air sitting on top of the cold air would boost the exit speed of the air.  Also you could have the Darwin or leaves sit on the entrance point.  The moving air also creates a vacuum which helps move the air along.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @elizabethmckinney7182 it's a good idea but it is a harder make to do that - if I want a couple o fhundred of these they have to be as easy as possible - or they just won't get made - there is a cost to that in terms of collection - but nothing Is collected if nothing is made - the thing I think to strive for is simplicity of process as long as the efficiency cost isn't too high - more often than not folks try to get more and more efficient and the build cost goes up through the roof very very quickly - or they exceed their skill level - so many good ideas drown in that particular muddy pool

replies.author: @elizabethmckinney7182

author: @easy_s3351
text: Years ago (around 2012) I worked with a guy who was developing energy trees which would have leafs that would use photo-voltaic, thermo-voltaic and piezo technology to produce energy. Especially the last, piezo, was interesting as it produces energy from movement. If I remember correctly his company was called Solar Botanic, not sure if anything ever came from it. But if you're looking at wind leafs that might be the way to go, using the movement of the leafs to produce energy rather than trying to use them to capture the wind to drive a turbine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think know it mate - If memory serves there was a plan to put it in the boulevards of Paris - but it came to nothing in the end

replies.author: @elizabethmckinney7182
replies.text: The problem with that project was the cost of production. This wind harvester has few moving parts and is just as effective... So less maintenance and very low production cost. And literally anyone can 3d print it.

Jus sayin. Thanx in advance for your consideration!
